title: Moore’s Law Destroys the Paradox of Thrift
body_html:
If people really stopped buying whenever they expected lower prices later, nobody would ever have purchased a computer. That is the devastating simplicity of the essay’s Moore’s Law argument. Computing has been one of the most durable and visible deflationary sectors in modern life, and yet demand did not collapse. It exploded.

Why? Because lower prices did not make computing irrelevant. They made computing more useful, more widespread, and more deeply embedded in everything else. Falling costs opened new applications, new business models, and new layers of productive possibility. Deflation did not suppress demand; it multiplied the contexts in which demand became rational.

That is a broader lesson about technology. People do not buy only according to abstract price trajectories. They buy according to usefulness, urgency, competition, status, productivity, and the alternatives available right now. A cheaper future product does not erase the current value of a product that changes what you can do today.

Moore’s Law is therefore a standing empirical refutation of simplistic anti-deflation stories. The world’s most famous long-running cost decline did not produce starvation or stagnation. It produced the digital age.
